Headline: The Smartphone
Text: From the first handheld call to the chip war: the smartphone as a geopolitical object — cobalt from Congo, assembly across Asia, and a fight over who controls the stack. A story by MICRI group 5.

Headline: The Birth of Mobile Communication
Text: Martin Cooper of Motorola made the world's first handheld cellular phone call. This breakthrough positioned the United States as the pioneer of mobile telecommunications during the Cold War and marked the beginning of a technological race that would later become a key component of economic and strategic power.
Media Caption: Martin Cooper, on April 3, 1973, during the first mobile phone call.
Alt Text: Martin Cooper holding the first handheld mobile phone
Source: Burchett, M. H. (2021). First cellular phone call. EBSCO Research Starters.

Headline: Commercial Launch of IBM Simon
Text: The first commercially available smartphone introduced touchscreen technology, email, and digital productivity tools. Although commercially limited, it demonstrated the growing importance of software and digital services as strategic assets in technological development.
Media Caption: Pictured in its charging base, the IBM Simon Personal Communicator is widely considered to be the world's first smartphone.

Headline: Europe Takes the Lead in Mobile Technology
Text: Nokia became the world's leading mobile phone manufacturer, overtaking Motorola and capturing 22.9% of the global mobile phone market. Its success demonstrated that innovation in telecommunications was not exclusively American and highlighted the strategic importance of mobile infrastructure in the emerging digital economy.
Media Caption: A classic Nokia handset from the era when the Finnish company overtook Motorola to become the world's leading mobile phone manufacturer. 1998
Source: Nokia Corporation. (1999). Nokia Business Review 1998. Nokia Corporation.

Headline: The Smartphone Enters Government and Business
Text: BlackBerry revolutionized mobile communication through secure email services. Its devices became standard tools for governments, diplomats, military officials, and multinational corporations, emphasizing the growing link between communication technologies, cybersecurity, and national security.
Media Caption: The vintage BlackBerry 850 device on display in a museum.
Source: Radov, M. (2019). The rise and fall of Research in Motion. Ivey Business School.

Headline: Taiwan Joins the Global Tech Race
Text: HTC's growth reflected Taiwan's emergence as a critical player in global electronics manufacturing. This period marked the beginning of Taiwan's central role in the smartphone supply chain, which would later become strategically important through semiconductor production.
Source: Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). HTC. In Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.

Headline: The Hidden Cost of Smartphone Batteries
Text: The rapid expansion of smartphones increased demand for lithium-ion batteries, making cobalt a critical resource. The Democratic Republic of the Congo supplies most of the world's cobalt, exposing global manufacturers to geopolitical risks, governance challenges, and concerns regarding labor exploitation and human rights.
Media Caption: Artisanal mine in Kailo, Congo - Image released under Creative Commons license
Source: Gulley, A. L. (2022). One hundred years of cobalt production in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Resources Policy, 79, 103007.

Headline: China's Grip on Critical Minerals
Text: China established a dominant position in the extraction and processing of rare earth elements essential for smartphones, batteries, and electronics. This control provided Beijing with significant strategic leverage over global technology industries and highlighted the geopolitical importance of critical raw materials.
Source: International Energy Agency. (2026). Rare Earth Elements. Paris: IEA.

Headline: The iPhone Revolution
Text: Steve Jobs introduced the iPhone, transforming smartphones into multifunctional digital platforms. The device re-established U.S. technological leadership and accelerated the rise of platform-based ecosystems, giving American companies unprecedented influence over global communication, commerce, and data flows
Media Caption: Steve Jobs introduces the first iphone on stage, 2007.

Headline: The Rise of the Open Smartphone Ecosystem
Text: Google's Android operating system created an open mobile ecosystem adopted by manufacturers worldwide. While reinforcing U.S. dominance in software and digital services, it also enabled the rapid expansion of Asian hardware producers, contributing to a more interconnected global technology industry.

Headline: Rare Earth Crisis
Text: During a territorial dispute, China temporarily restricted rare earth exports to Japan. The incident demonstrated how control over critical resources could be used as a geopolitical tool and prompted many countries to reconsider their dependence on Chinese supply chains.
Source: OECD. (2024). Trade impacts of economic coercion (OECD Trade Policy Papers No. 281). OECD Publishing.

Headline: South Korea's Technological Powerhouse Emerges
Text: Samsung's rise reflected South Korea's transformation into a technological powerhouse. Through vertical integration in semiconductors, displays, and consumer electronics, the company strengthened South Korea's position within global value chains and technological innovation networks.
Media Caption: The Galaxy S II: a product of Samsung's vertical integration in semiconductors, displays, and consumer electronics. 2011
Source: Samsung Electronics. (2015, April 3). The five-year journey of the Samsung Galaxy S.

Headline: China Challenges Western Tech Dominance
Text: Chinese smartphone manufacturers rapidly gained global market share through competitive pricing, innovation, and state-supported industrial development. Their success symbolized China's transition from a manufacturing center to a major technological competitor challenging Western dominance.
Source: World Intellectual Property Organization. (2019, March 19). Innovators file record number of international patent applications, with Asia now leading.

Headline: Scrutiny of Smartphone Supply Chains
Text: Increased awareness of labor exploitation, environmental degradation, and unsustainable mining practices led governments, NGOs, and consumers to demand greater transparency and corporate responsibility. Smartphone production became a prominent example of the challenges associated with globalization.
Source: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. (2019). Interconnected supply chains: A comprehensive look at due diligence challenges and opportunities sourcing cobalt and copper from the Democratic Republic of the Congo. OECD Publishing.

Headline: The U.S.–China Tech War Begins
Text: The United States imposed restrictions on Huawei over security concerns, limiting access to American technologies and semiconductor components. The dispute became one of the clearest examples of the growing technological decoupling between the United States and China and the strategic importance of digital infrastructure.
Source: Congressional Research Service. (2022). U.S. restrictions on Huawei Technologies: National security, foreign policy, and economic interests (CRS Report No. R47012).

Headline: Taiwan: The Silicon Heart of the World
Text: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) became the world's leading producer of advanced semiconductors. As smartphones increasingly depend on cutting-edge chips, Taiwan emerged as one of the most strategically significant territories in the global economy and in U.S.–China relations
Media Caption: The TSMC logo on the facade of a factory building in Taichung, Taiwan
Source: Haramboure, A., Lalanne, G., Schwellnus, C., & Guilhoto, J. (2023). Vulnerabilities in the semiconductor supply chain (OECD Science, Technology and Industry Working Papers No. 2023/05). OECD Publishing.

Headline: Competition for Technological Autonomy
Text: Governments increasingly view semiconductors, batteries, and critical minerals as matters of national security. Large-scale industrial policies such as the U.S. CHIPS Act and the European Chips Act aim to reduce dependence on foreign suppliers and strengthen technological sovereignty.
Source: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. (2026). National security considerations in competition enforcement. OECD.

Headline: The AI Smartphone Era
Text: Smartphones are becoming key platforms for artificial intelligence applications, including generative AI, voice assistants, and personalized services. Competition is shifting from hardware to control over data, algorithms, and computing power, creating new geopolitical dynamics around digital sovereignty.

Headline: The Fight for Sustainable Technology
Text: The European Union has introduced regulations requiring longer device lifespans, repairability, and easier battery replacement. These measures aim to reduce electronic waste, decrease dependence on imported critical materials, and position the EU as a global regulatory leader in sustainable technology governance.
